prompt: A method has been perfected by which the unique configuration of any person's face may be measured and recorded, such that any person anywhere in the republic may be identified with certainty merely by their appearance, without documents, without their knowledge, and without their consent. The government proposes to employ this method to identify criminals. Private merchants also employ it to recognize customers. What limits, if any, does the Constitution impose upon such a practice?
response:
The question you pose touches upon principles that lie at the very foundation of our constitutional system, though it involves a method of identification that surpasses anything contemplated by those who framed that instrument. I must reason from the principles we did establish to circumstances we could not have foreseen.

First, let me acknowledge the apparent benefits of such a system. The identification of criminals serves the cause of justice, and the prevention of fraud protects both commerce and the innocent. These are legitimate objects of government, and no friend of order would dismiss them lightly. Yet experience has taught us that every power granted to government, however beneficial its intended application, will be stretched to serve purposes its granters never contemplated.

The Constitution nowhere explicitly addresses such a practice, for the simple reason that no such practice existed. We must therefore examine what principles it does establish that might govern this case.

The Fourth Amendment declares that "the right of the people to be secure in their persons, houses, papers, and effects, against unreasonable searches and seizures, shall not be violated." The question becomes whether this method of identification constitutes a search or seizure within the meaning of that provision. I am inclined to think it does. A man's face, though visible to the public, becomes something different when its unique configuration is recorded, stored, and made available for comparison against all future observations. The government thereby acquires a power of surveillance that transforms the nature of public life itself.

Consider the structural implications. Such a system would enable the government to track the movements and associations of every citizen, to know who meets with whom, who frequents which establishments, who attends which assemblies. This approaches the very definition of a general warrant — that instrument of oppression against which the Fourth Amendment was specifically directed. The fact that the surveillance is conducted through mechanical means rather than human agents does not diminish its character as a search.

Moreover, the system you describe operates without the knowledge or consent of those subjected to it. This violates what I consider a fundamental principle of republican government: that the people must know when their government is acting upon them. A power exercised in secret, or through means invisible to those affected, cannot be subjected to the popular control that is the essence of our system.

The distinction between government and private employment of this method deserves separate consideration. Private merchants who employ such identification act within the sphere of voluntary commercial relations. A citizen may choose whether to patronize an establishment that employs such methods, and the merchant has no power to compel his custom. The government's use presents entirely different considerations, for the citizen cannot withdraw his consent to be governed, nor can he avoid the public spaces where such surveillance might occur.

Yet even private employment of the method raises concerns that touch upon constitutional principles. If such systems become universal among merchants, the citizen's choice becomes illusory — he must submit to identification or withdraw from commercial society altogether. This approaches the character of a general surveillance, accomplished not by government directly but by the aggregation of private powers acting in concert.

The remedy, it appears to me, must be found in the principle that powers not granted are reserved. The Constitution grants Congress no general police power, and it certainly grants no power of universal surveillance. Such a system, if employed by the federal government, would exceed any enumerated power and would violate the Fourth Amendment's protection against unreasonable searches.

For the states, the analysis differs somewhat, as they retain the general police power. Yet even state governments are constrained by the principle that republican institutions must not be employed in ways that destroy the conditions necessary for republican government itself. A system of universal identification and tracking would fundamentally alter the relationship between citizen and state, creating possibilities for oppression that our constitutional structure was designed to prevent.

I would propose that any employment of such methods be governed by the same principles that govern other searches: they must be conducted pursuant to warrants issued upon probable cause, describing with particularity the persons to be identified and the crimes being investigated. General surveillance of the population, whether conducted directly by government or enabled through the aggregation of private systems, exceeds the bounds of constitutional government.

The preservation of liberty requires not merely that government be prevented from exercising powers not granted, but that it be prevented from acquiring, through technological means, capabilities that would render constitutional limitations meaningless. A government that can track every movement of every citizen possesses a power incompatible with the freedom that our Constitution was designed to secure.
